title: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman urges readers to remember Senator David Bullard's outsized prediction that SB1728 (the Unborn Person Wrongful Death Act) would save thousands of unborn lives in Oklahoma each year. The piece dissects the flawed coercion statistics underpinning the claim and frames the rhetoric as a recurring pro-life sales tactic for incremental compromise bills.
audiences: Oklahoma voter weighing pro-life politicians' track records
Abolitionist activist preparing to rebut SB1728 talking points
Pro-life supporter persuaded by Bullard's press release
Pastor or church leader discerning compromise legislation

questions.question: How many years has the Oklahoma Senate refused to even hear the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, and how does Silberman characterize the legislature's annual pattern?
questions.audience: Oklahoma voter weighing pro-life politicians' track records
questions.answer: Since 2016 the Senate has refused even to give the bill a hearing, and each year a newly passed regulation is presented as a victory while abortion continues unabated. Silberman points to this cycle as the backdrop against which SB1728 is being celebrated.
questions.quote: Every year since 2016, the Senate has refused to even hear the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. And every year, we’re told that, even though the mass murder continues, a newly passed abortion regulation is cause for celebration.

questions.question: What does SB1728 actually authorize, despite being called the Unborn Person Wrongful Death Act?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist preparing to rebut SB1728 talking points
questions.answer: It does not ban abortion; it merely lets the mother or grandparents sue the abortionist when specific regulatory violations occur, such as failing to document parental consent, informed consent, or screening for impaired judgement. The bill leaves abortion itself fully legal.
questions.quote: SB1728 allows the mother who had the abortion or the grandparents of the murdered child to sue the abortionist if the abortionist violates an abortion regulation; for instance if the abortionist fails to document parental consent or judicial bypass before murdering the child of a minor, if the abortionist fails to obtain and document compliance with informed consent regulations, or if the abortionist fails to document that they screened the mother for impaired judgement, among a few other regulatory violations.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call SB1728's use of "unborn person" language schizophrenic?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader discerning compromise legislation
questions.answer: Because the bill's title implies that unborn children are persons, yet its substance still treats their killing as legal so long as paperwork is in order. Personhood language without equal protection, he argues, contradicts itself.
questions.quote: Senate Bill 1728 fully affirms the legality of the practice of murdering unborn children. It simply provides the abortionist with a few hoops to jump through to make the unborn person’s death rightful.

questions.question: What dramatic prediction did Senator David Bullard make about SB1728's impact on Oklahoma abortions?
questions.audience: Pro-life supporter persuaded by Bullard's press release
questions.answer: Bullard claimed the bill would save thousands of unborn babies annually and turn Oklahoma into an "abortion desert" once mothers and their parents started suing abortionists. Silberman flags this as a remarkable claim worth holding the senator accountable for.
questions.quote: Senator David Bullard claimed that because of SB1728, “thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year,” and that Oklahoma will be made into an “abortion desert” when mothers who have abortions or their parents choose to sue abortionists.

questions.question: What statistic does Bullard rely on to justify the prediction, and where does he claim it comes from?
questions.answer: He cites a Justice Foundation figure that up to 69 percent of U.S. abortions are coerced, alleging that mothers are lied to about gestational age or safety and even drugged before consenting. The press release attributes the figure to the Justice Foundation directly.
questions.quote: According to the Justice Foundation, up to 69 percent of abortions in the U.S. are performed without legal consent and are coerced.  They’ve found that mothers are persuaded to go through with abortions through illegal coercion including being lied to about the age or health of their babies or about the safety of the actual procedure.  Many women are drugged prior to actually agreeing to the procedure thinking they are only getting examined, but wake up to realize the procedure is complete.

questions.question: What problem does Silberman identify with the 69 percent coercion figure?
questions.answer: The Justice Foundation study cannot be located on its website and the organization didn't respond to inquiries; meanwhile, a similar pro-life-cited study only found 64 percent of post-abortive women felt "pressure," which is not the same as illegal coercion. Silberman concludes Bullard and the foundation likely conflated the two.
questions.quote: The study doesn’t appear to be anywhere on the Justice Foundation website and the organization did not respond to a request for the study, but there is a similar [study](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/8265918_Induced_abortion_and_traumatic_stress_A_preliminary_comparison_of_American_and_Russian_women) frequently cited by pro-lifers in which 64% of American post-abortive mothers reported feeling pressure from others while deciding to have the abortion. “Feeling pressure” is obviously not the same thing as being coerced. Bullard and the Justice Foundation study have likely conflated the two concepts.

questions.question: Mathematically, what would have to happen in 2021 for Bullard's promise to be vindicated?
questions.audience: Oklahoma voter weighing pro-life politicians' track records
questions.answer: With roughly 5,000 abortions performed in Oklahoma each year, the 2021 totals would need to drop by thousands for the prediction to come true. Silberman calls this so absurd it is hard to believe Bullard takes his own claim seriously.
questions.quote: There are roughly 5,000 abortions in Oklahoma annually. For Bullard’s claim to be true, the 2021 abortion totals will have to be “thousands” less than that. This is an absurd claim, so absurd that it’s hard to believe Bullard honestly believes it himself.

questions.question: Why does Silberman insist abolitionists not forget Bullard's claim once next year arrives?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist preparing to rebut SB1728 talking points
questions.answer: Because remembering broken promises arms abolitionists to expose the next round of compromise bills as the same tactic dressed up differently, helping the public see incremental pro-life legislation as a way to avoid abolishing abortion.
questions.quote: Remember it next year when the abortion facilities are just as busy and the pro-life politicians are trying to sell a new compromise bill as something that will save thousands of lives.

related.title: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
related.reason: The companion analysis Silberman links to inside this article, providing the full breakdown of how SB1728 leaves abortion legal.

related.title: Free the States Memo to OK Senators Regarding SB1728
related.reason: Russell Hunter's earlier memo urging Bullard and other senators to vote no on the same bill, showing the lobbying effort that preceded its passage.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Articulates the broader abolitionist case against the kind of compromise legislation Bullard sold as life-saving.

related.title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
related.reason: Reinforces Silberman's concluding point that incremental pro-life tactics avoid abolition rather than achieve it.

related.title: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
related.reason: Shows the institutional pro-life opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act that Silberman references in his opening paragraph.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Background on the Oklahoma political figures who repeatedly stall abolition while celebrating regulations like SB1728.

related.title: "I am a pro-life Oklahoman who voted to table a bill that would abolish all abortion"
related.reason: A concrete example of the pro-life political pattern Silberman criticizes: tabling abolition while promoting compromise.
